joel_day.title: The Locust Plague -- YHWH's Advance Army
joel_day.synopsis: Joel opens with a catastrophic locust invasion described in four stages: 'What the cutting locust left, the swarming locust has eaten. What the swarming locust left, the hopping locust has eaten. What the hopping locust left, the destroying locust has eaten' (1:4). Four Hebrew words for locust -- gazam, arbeh, yeleq, hasil -- describe wave after wave of devastation. The nation's food supply is destroyed: grain, wine, and oil (the covenant blessings of Deut 7:13). Joel calls the drunkards to weep (no wine), the priests to mourn (no grain or drink offerings), the farmers to despair (fields stripped bare). The locust plague is 'a nation has come up against my land, powerful and beyond number' (1:6) -- deliberately described in military terms, foreshadowing the Day of YHWH in chapter 2. Joel calls for a solemn assembly, fasting, and crying out to YHWH: 'Alas for the day! For the day of YHWH is near' (1:15). The agricultural devastation is total: even the beasts of the field groan, and the water brooks have dried up.

key_verse.text: Alas for the day! For the day of the LORD is near, and as destruction from the Almighty it comes.

hebrew_terms: gazam (cutting/gnawing locust -- the first wave of destruction)
arbeh (swarming locust -- the massive swarm; the most common Hebrew word for locust)
yeleq (hopping/licking locust -- the larval stage that consumes what remains)
hasil (destroying/consuming locust -- the final wave that strips everything bare)
yom YHWH (Day of YHWH -- introduced in 1:15; the controlling eschatological concept)
Shaddai (the Almighty -- 'as destruction from Shaddai'; wordplay: shod/Shaddai)
joel_day.ane_backdrop: Locust plagues were among the most feared natural disasters in the ancient Near East. Egyptian, Mesopotamian, and Ugaritic texts describe their devastating impact on agriculture. The parallels to the eighth plague of Egypt (Exod 10:1-20) are intentional: Joel frames the locust invasion as YHWH's judgment on his own people, just as the locusts were judgment on Egypt. The four Hebrew terms for locust may represent developmental stages of the same species or different species arriving in successive waves.

second_temple.summary: John's fifth trumpet: locust-like creatures from the abyss with scorpion stingers, commanded not to kill but to torment for five months.
second_temple.relevance: John transforms Joel's literal locust plague into an apocalyptic vision of demonic forces -- locusts as spiritual judgment on the unrepentant.

cross_refs.note: The eighth plague of Egypt: locusts -- Joel's plague echoes the exodus judgment
Covenant blessings of grain, wine, and oil -- precisely what the locusts destroy
Amos's warning: the Day of YHWH is darkness, not light -- Joel develops this further
Apocalyptic locust army -- Joel's imagery transformed into spiritual warfare

joel_day.divine_council_note: Joel's locust army is described as a 'nation' that 'has come up against my land' (1:6). In chapter 2, this army becomes explicitly YHWH's: 'his army' (2:11). In the divine council framework, YHWH commands both natural and supernatural forces. The locusts are agents of divine council judgment -- not random natural disaster but directed punishment for covenant violation.

sections.heading: The Four Waves of Destruction (1:1-4)
sections.body: Joel calls the elders and all inhabitants to hear an unprecedented event: wave after wave of locusts has consumed everything. The four stages emphasize totality -- nothing escapes. The elders are asked: 'Has such a thing happened in your days, or in the days of your fathers?' This is a crisis without precedent.

sections.heading: The Call to Mourn (1:5-14)
sections.body: Drunkards, priests, and farmers are all summoned to lament. The vine is laid waste, the fig tree stripped bare. The grain and drink offerings are cut off from the Temple -- even YHWH's worship is disrupted. Joel calls for a solemn assembly: 'Consecrate a fast; call a solemn assembly... and cry out to YHWH' (1:14).

sections.heading: The Day of YHWH Is Near (1:15-20)
sections.body: Joel interprets the locust plague theologically: 'The day of YHWH is near, and as destruction from Shaddai it comes' (1:15). The Hebrew wordplay shod/Shaddai ('destruction from the Almighty') underscores that this is divine judgment. Even the beasts of the field cry out, and fire has consumed the pastures. The natural disaster is a preview of the eschatological Day.

joel_day.title: The Locust Army, Repentance, and Restoration
joel_day.synopsis: Chapter 2 is the theological center of the locust plague narrative. It opens with the alarm trumpet: 'Blow the trumpet in Zion; sound the alarm on my holy mountain!' (2:1). The locust army is described in terrifying military terms: 'a great and powerful people; their like has never been nor will be again' (2:2). They march in formation, scale walls, and cannot be stopped. 'The earth quakes before them; the heavens tremble. The sun and the moon are darkened, and the stars withdraw their shining' (2:10). This is 'his army' -- YHWH commands the locust host. Then the pivot: 'Yet even now, return to me with all your heart, with fasting, with weeping, and with mourning; and rend your hearts and not your garments' (2:12-13). YHWH responds to repentance with restoration: 'I will restore to you the years that the swarming locust has eaten' (2:25). 'You shall know that I am in the midst of Israel, and that I am YHWH your God and there is none else' (2:27).

key_verse.text: "Yet even now," declares the LORD, "return to me with all your heart, with fasting, with weeping, and with mourning; and rend your hearts and not your garments." Return to the LORD your God, for he is gracious and merciful, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love; and he relents over disaster.

hebrew_terms: shofar (trumpet/ram's horn -- the alarm call, eschatological signal)
shuv (return/repent -- the central call: 'return to me with all your heart')
channun verachum (gracious and merciful -- YHWH's character, echoing Exod 34:6-7)
nacham (relent -- 'he relents over disaster'; YHWH's response to genuine repentance)
qara (to call/assemble -- 'consecrate a fast, call a solemn assembly')
joel_day.ane_backdrop: The Day of YHWH concept has roots in ancient Near Eastern divine warrior mythology. When a god went to battle, cosmic phenomena accompanied the march: earthquake, darkness, celestial disturbance. Joel's description of darkened sun and quaking earth follows this theophanic warrior pattern. The trumpet (shofar) was both a military and liturgical instrument -- signaling war and calling to worship.

second_temple.summary: Jesus in the Olivet Discourse: 'The sun will be darkened, and the moon will not give its light, and the stars will fall from heaven.'
second_temple.relevance: Jesus applies Joel's cosmic signs to the eschatological events surrounding the Son of Man's return.

cross_refs.note: YHWH's self-revelation: 'gracious and merciful, slow to anger' -- Joel 2:13 quotes this directly
Moses: 'Would that all YHWH's people were prophets!' -- anticipates Joel's Spirit promise
Jonah cites the same Exod 34:6 formula -- the character of YHWH that grounds repentance
Cosmic signs from Joel -- sun darkened, moon to blood, stars falling
Sixth seal: 'the sun became black... the full moon became like blood' -- Joel imagery

joel_day.divine_council_note: YHWH 'utters his voice before his army' (2:11) -- the divine warrior leading his host in battle. The cosmic signs (darkened sun, trembling heavens) represent the host of heaven responding to YHWH's theophanic approach. Yet the same sovereign who commands the locust army offers restoration to those who repent. In the divine council framework, YHWH's judgment is never arbitrary: it serves the purpose of turning his people back to covenant faithfulness. The call to 'rend your hearts' is a call to genuine alignment with YHWH's purposes, not mere ritual performance.

sections.heading: The Locust Army: YHWH's Host (2:1-11)
sections.body: The trumpet sounds the alarm. The locusts are described as an invincible army: marching in ranks, scaling walls, entering houses. Cosmic signs accompany their advance: quaking earth, trembling heavens, darkened sun and moon. YHWH 'utters his voice before his army' (2:11) -- this is the divine warrior leading his host.

sections.heading: The Call to Repentance (2:12-17)
sections.body: Despite the terrifying army, YHWH offers a way out: 'Return to me with all your heart.' The basis for hope: 'He is gracious and merciful, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love; and he relents over disaster' (2:13, echoing Exod 34:6-7). 'Who knows whether he will not turn and relent?' The priests are to weep between the porch and the altar: 'Spare your people, O YHWH!'

sections.heading: Restoration: The Years Restored (2:18-27)
sections.body: YHWH responds with jealousy for his land and pity for his people. He will restore the grain, wine, and oil. He will remove the 'northerner' (the locust army or an invading force). 'I will restore to you the years that the swarming locust has eaten' (2:25). 'You shall know that I am in the midst of Israel, and that I am YHWH your God and there is none else' (2:27).

joel_spirit.title: The Spirit on All Flesh -- Pentecost Promised
joel_spirit.synopsis: After the locust judgment and restoration of Joel 2:1-27, the prophet pivots to the climactic promise: 'And it shall come to pass afterward, that I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy, your old men shall dream dreams, and your young men shall see visions. Even on the male and female servants in those days I will pour out my Spirit' (2:28-29). This is the democratization of the prophetic gift. In the Old Testament, the Spirit empowered specific individuals -- prophets, kings, judges. Joel promises universal access. Before the great and awesome Day of YHWH, cosmic signs appear: 'blood and fire and columns of smoke. The sun shall be turned to darkness, and the moon to blood' (2:30-31). Yet the passage culminates in grace: 'And it shall come to pass that everyone who calls on the name of YHWH shall be saved. For in Mount Zion and in Jerusalem there shall be those who escape, as YHWH has said, and among the survivors shall be those whom YHWH calls' (2:32). Peter identifies Pentecost as the fulfillment of this promise (Acts 2:16-21). Paul universalizes the salvation offer in Romans 10:13.

key_verse.text: And it shall come to pass afterward, that I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy, your old men shall dream dreams, and your young men shall see visions. Even on the male and female servants in those days I will pour out my Spirit.

hebrew_terms: ruach (Spirit/wind -- the divine presence poured out on all flesh)
kol basar (all flesh -- the universality of the Spirit promise, transcending all social boundaries)
naba (to prophesy -- sons and daughters will prophesy; prophetic access for all)
chalomot (dreams -- old men dream dreams; a mode of divine revelation, cf. Num 12:6)
chezyonot (visions -- young men see visions; prophetic sight given to the next generation)
qara beshem YHWH (to call on the name of YHWH -- the salvation condition; quoted by Paul in Rom 10:13)
joel_spirit.ane_backdrop: In the ancient Near East, the spirit of a deity was understood as the divine power that enabled humans to act beyond their natural capacity. In Israel, YHWH's Spirit empowered judges to deliver (Judg 3:10; 6:34), kings to rule (1 Sam 16:13), and prophets to speak (Ezek 2:2). The radical nature of Joel's promise is that this Spirit would be given to 'all flesh' -- without regard to age, gender, or social status. In a culture where prophecy was the province of a recognized prophetic class, this was revolutionary. Moses had wished for it (Num 11:29); Joel declares it will happen.

second_temple.summary: Peter at Pentecost: 'This is what was uttered through the prophet Joel: "In the last days... I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh."'
second_temple.relevance: The foundational New Testament text for Pentecost theology. Peter identifies the descent of the Spirit as the fulfillment of Joel 2:28-32, inaugurating the 'last days' in which all believers have prophetic access to God.

second_temple.summary: Paul quotes Joel 2:32: 'Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.'
second_temple.relevance: Paul applies Joel's salvation promise universally -- Jew and Gentile alike. The 'name of the Lord' now includes Jesus (Phil 2:9-11).

second_temple.summary: Paul: 'There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is no male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus.'
second_temple.relevance: Joel's Spirit promise already anticipated the breaking of social boundaries -- sons and daughters, old and young, male and female servants. Paul's baptismal formula echoes this leveling effect of the Spirit.

cross_refs.note: Moses: 'Would that all YHWH's people were prophets!' -- Joel 2:28 fulfills this wish
'Until the Spirit is poured upon us from on high' -- the same eschatological Spirit outpouring
'I will put my Spirit within you' -- the new covenant promise of inward transformation
Peter quotes Joel 2:28-32 at Pentecost -- the Spirit on all flesh fulfilled
Paul quotes Joel 2:32 -- universal salvation for all who call on the name of the Lord
Sixth seal: sun darkened, moon to blood -- Joel's cosmic signs in the apocalyptic vision

joel_spirit.divine_council_note: The Spirit outpouring of Joel 2:28-29 is a divine council democratization. In the Old Testament, the Spirit empowered specific individuals to participate in YHWH's purposes -- prophets who stood in the divine council (Jer 23:18, 22), kings anointed for rule, judges empowered for deliverance. Joel promises that this access will extend to 'all flesh' -- sons, daughters, old, young, servants. At Pentecost, this promise is fulfilled: every believer receives the Spirit and thus has prophetic access to YHWH's council deliberations. The cosmic signs (darkened sun, blood moon) represent the host of heaven responding to YHWH's theophanic approach -- the divine council members dim before the glory of the Most High.

sections.heading: The Spirit on All Flesh (2:28-29)
sections.body: The climactic promise: 'afterward' -- after the locust judgment and restoration -- YHWH will pour out his Spirit on all flesh. Sons and daughters, old and young, male and female servants will all prophesy. Every social boundary is crossed: age, gender, and class. Moses' wish (Num 11:29) becomes YHWH's eschatological decree.

sections.heading: Cosmic Signs Before the Day (2:30-31)
sections.body: Before the great and awesome Day of YHWH: blood, fire, columns of smoke. The sun turns to darkness and the moon to blood. These cosmic disturbances signal the approach of the divine warrior. The celestial order itself responds to YHWH's coming -- the heavenly luminaries bow before their maker.

sections.heading: Everyone Who Calls Shall Be Saved (2:32)
sections.body: The salvation condition is simple and universal: 'Everyone who calls on the name of YHWH shall be saved.' Deliverance is in Mount Zion and Jerusalem -- the place of YHWH's presence. Among the survivors are 'those whom YHWH calls' -- salvation is both human response (calling) and divine initiative (calling). Peter and Paul both cite this verse as the foundation of gospel proclamation.

joel_spirit.title: The Valley of Jehoshaphat -- YHWH Judges the Nations
joel_spirit.synopsis: Joel's final chapter is a full-scale divine council judgment scene. YHWH declares: 'I will gather all the nations and bring them down to the Valley of Jehoshaphat. And I will enter into judgment with them there, on behalf of my people and my heritage Israel, because they have scattered them among the nations and have divided up my land' (3:2). The specific charges: Tyre, Sidon, and Philistia sold Judah's children to the Greeks as slaves. The verdict: 'I will return your payment on your own head' (3:4). The nations are summoned to war: 'Beat your plowshares into swords, and your pruning hooks into spears' (3:10 -- the deliberate reversal of Isaiah 2:4 and Micah 4:3). This is not peace but final conflict. The harvest imagery of judgment: 'Put in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe. Go in, tread, for the winepress is full. The vats overflow, for their evil is great' (3:13). YHWH roars from Zion, heaven and earth shake, but 'YHWH is a refuge to his people' (3:16). The book closes with an Edenic vision: 'The mountains shall drip sweet wine, and the hills shall flow with milk... a fountain shall come forth from the house of YHWH' (3:18).

key_verse.text: The LORD roars from Zion, and utters his voice from Jerusalem, and the heavens and the earth quake. But the LORD is a refuge to his people, a stronghold to the people of Israel.

hebrew_terms: emeq Yehoshaphat (Valley of Jehoshaphat, 'YHWH judges' -- the site of eschatological judgment)
shaphat (to judge -- YHWH as cosmic judge; the root embedded in 'Jehoshaphat')
qatsir (harvest -- judgment as harvest; the grain is ripe, the winepress full)
gath (winepress -- 'the winepress is full'; blood imagery of final judgment)
sha'ag (to roar -- 'YHWH roars from Zion'; the divine warrior's battle cry)
ma'on (refuge -- YHWH as refuge for his people amidst cosmic upheaval)
joel_spirit.ane_backdrop: The Valley of Jehoshaphat may be the Kidron Valley between Jerusalem and the Mount of Olives, or it may be a symbolic name meaning 'YHWH judges.' The harvest-as-judgment imagery draws on ancient Near Eastern threshing-floor symbolism, where grain was separated from chaff -- a natural metaphor for divine sorting of righteous and wicked. The slave trade in Jewish captives by Tyre, Sidon, and Philistia is attested in Amos 1:6-10 and Ezekiel 27. The reversal of Isaiah's plowshares-to-swords vision is a deliberate prophetic inversion: when the nations refuse peace, they are summoned to the war they chose.

second_temple.summary: John's vision of the grain harvest and the winepress of God's wrath, drawn directly from Joel 3:13.
second_temple.relevance: John transforms Joel's harvest imagery into the final eschatological judgment: the Son of Man reaps the earth, and blood flows from the winepress.

second_temple.summary: The sheep-and-goats judgment: 'When the Son of Man comes in his glory... he will separate the nations.'
second_temple.relevance: Jesus' judgment of the nations echoes Joel 3's gathering of nations for judgment based on how they treated YHWH's people.

second_temple.summary: The river of the water of life flowing from the throne of God, with the tree of life bearing fruit.
second_temple.relevance: Joel 3:18's fountain flowing from the house of YHWH finds its ultimate fulfillment in Revelation's vision of the renewed Eden in the New Jerusalem.

cross_refs.note: 'They shall beat their swords into plowshares' -- Joel 3:10 deliberately reverses this
Micah's plowshares vision -- Joel's reversal signals final war, not peace
The river flowing from the Temple -- echoed in Joel 3:18's fountain from YHWH's house
Amos indicts Gaza and Tyre for the same slave trade Joel condemns
The harvest of judgment and the winepress of God's wrath
The Son of Man judges the nations -- Joel's vision developed christologically
'He will tread the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God' -- Joel 3:13

joel_spirit.divine_council_note: Joel 3 is an explicit divine council judgment scene. YHWH gathers the nations to the Valley of Jehoshaphat ('YHWH judges') and enters into judgment (shaphat) with them. The charges are specific: the nations have scattered YHWH's people, divided YHWH's land, and sold YHWH's children into slavery. In the divine council framework, this is the eschatological reversal of the Deuteronomy 32 allotment: the nations allotted to the sons of God are now judged for their mistreatment of YHWH's inheritance (Israel). YHWH roars from Zion -- the divine warrior acts from his earthly throne, and heaven and earth respond. The judgment imagery (harvest, winepress) represents the divine council's final verdict on the rebellious nations and their spiritual patrons. The Edenic closing vision (mountains dripping wine, fountain from YHWH's house) signals the restoration of the divine order: YHWH dwelling in Zion, his people secure, creation flourishing under his direct rule.

sections.heading: The Nations Gathered for Judgment (3:1-8)
sections.body: YHWH will restore Judah and Jerusalem and simultaneously gather the nations for judgment. The charges: scattering Israel, dividing YHWH's land, selling children into slavery. Tyre, Sidon, and Philistia are specifically indicted. The sentence is measure for measure: their own children will be sold to distant nations.

sections.heading: Plowshares into Swords -- The Final Battle (3:9-13)
sections.body: Joel reverses Isaiah's and Micah's peace vision: 'Beat your plowshares into swords!' The nations are summoned to war against YHWH -- but it is a trap. 'Let the nations stir themselves up and come to the Valley of Jehoshaphat; for there I will sit to judge all the surrounding nations' (3:12). The harvest is ripe, the winepress full -- YHWH treads the grapes of wrath.

sections.heading: YHWH Roars from Zion (3:14-16)
sections.body: Multitudes in the valley of decision. Sun and moon darkened, stars dim. YHWH roars from Zion -- the divine warrior's battle cry. Heaven and earth quake. But for his people, YHWH is a refuge and stronghold.

sections.heading: Edenic Restoration (3:17-21)
sections.body: After judgment: 'Jerusalem shall be holy, and strangers shall never again pass through it.' The mountains drip wine, hills flow with milk, a fountain comes from YHWH's house. Egypt and Edom become desolate, but Judah endures forever. 'YHWH dwells in Zion' -- the final word is divine presence restored.
